summary: I am Rene Watt Lemaire of Battle Mountain, Nevada, and this is where I currently reside. This place is important to me as it is the setting for recording not only my life but also the stories of my grandparents. Battle Mountain serves as the backdrop for preserving the history of the Lemaire and Watt families.

My grandfather, George Watt, Sr., was born in 1831 in Dunfermline, Fife County, Scotland, and passed away on January 5, 1899. He started his career as a cabin boy on ships owned by a captain who admired his seamanship. When gold was discovered in California, he left the sea and traveled across the United States from New York to the California gold fields. He was very involved with livestock, running mule trains from Sacramento to the Mother Lode and Grass Valley, and he managed to make a decent fortune.

My grandfather George Watt, Sr. came from Scotland, specifically from Dunfermline in Fife County. Scotland was his homeland before he ventured out to seek opportunities abroad. His Scottish roots influenced his early life and career before he emigrated to America during the mid-19th century.

Dunfermline is the city in Fife County, Scotland, where my grandfather George Watt, Sr. was born in 1831. It’s a place close to Edinburgh and was his hometown before he left for America. Dunfermline represents the starting point of his journey from a young cabin boy to a man who sought fortune in California.

Fife County in Scotland is where Dunfermline is located, the birthplace of my grandfather George Watt, Sr. This region was part of his early life and heritage before he emigrated. Fife is important as it connects to the Scottish origins of my family.

Edinburgh is a city near Dunfermline in Fife County, Scotland, where my grandfather George Watt, Sr. was born. Though he didn’t live there, it helps to geographically place his origins in Scotland. Edinburgh is part of the Scottish landscape that shaped my grandfather’s early years.

My grandmother was Isabel Litster, who married my grandfather George Watt, Sr. She was part of the Watt family lineage that I am recounting. Though I don’t have many details about her, she was an important figure in my family’s history.

The California Gold Rush was a pivotal event that changed my grandfather George Watt, Sr.'s life. Around the time gold was discovered in California, he decided to leave his career at sea and head west. He traveled from New York City across the United States to the gold fields, which led him to become involved in livestock and mule trains in the Mother Lode region.

My grandfather George Watt, Sr. arrived in New York City after leaving the sea and before heading west to California. He got off the boat there and then worked his way across the country to the gold fields. New York City was the entry point for his journey into the American West during the Gold Rush era.

Sacramento, California, was a key location for my grandfather George Watt, Sr. during his time in the West. He ran mule trains from Sacramento up into the Mother Lode country, including Grass Valley. Sacramento was a hub for his livestock activities and part of his success in the region.

The Mother Lode was the gold region where my grandfather George Watt, Sr. ran mule trains carrying livestock from Sacramento. It was part of the California gold fields where he made his fortune. The Mother Lode area, including places like Grass Valley, was central to his work and life after arriving in California.

Grass Valley, California, was one of the towns in the Mother Lode region where my grandfather George Watt, Sr. operated mule trains. He was active in the livestock business there, which contributed to his financial success. Grass Valley represents an important part of his life during the California Gold Rush era.
